annotator S.report_text:
COZY BEAR's preferred intrusion method is a broadly targeted spearphish campaign that typically includes web links to a malicious dropper Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious link in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.

COZY BEAR's preferred intrusion method is a broadly targeted spearphish campaign that typically includes web links to a malicious dropper An adversary may rely upon a user clicking a malicious link in order to gain execution.

the code will deliver one of a number of sophisticated Remote Access Tools (RATs), including AdobeARM, ATI-Agent, and MiniDionis An adversary may use legitimate desktop support and remote access software to establish an interactive command and control channel to target systems within networks.

They have extensive checks for the various security software that is installed on the system and their specific configurations. Attackers perform extensive checks to identify the security software installed on the system and their specific configurations.

ensure the sample is not being analyzed on a virtual machine, using a debugger, or located within a sandbox Adversaries may employ various means to detect and avoid debuggers.

ensure the sample is not being analyzed on a virtual machine, using a debugger, or located within a sandbox Adversaries may employ various means to detect and avoid virtualization and analysis environments. This may include changing behaviors based on the results of checks for the presence of artifacts indicative of a virtual machine environment (VME) or sandbox.

An HTTP protocol with encrypted payload is used for the Command & Control communication. Adversaries may communicate using OSI application layer protocols.

The COZY BEAR intrusion relied primarily on the SeaDaddy implant developed in Python and compiled with py2exe and another Powershell backdoor with persistence accomplished via Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) system, which allowed the adversary to launch malicious code automatically after a specified period of system uptime or on a specific schedule. Adversaries may abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution.

The COZY BEAR intrusion relied primarily on the SeaDaddy implant developed in Python and compiled with py2exe and another Powershell backdoor with persistence accomplished via Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) system, which allowed the adversary to launch malicious code automatically after a specified period of system uptime or on a specific schedule. Adversaries may abuse Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to execute malicious commands and payloads.

It consists of a single obfuscated command setup to run persistently Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

establishes an encrypted connection to C2 Adversaries may employ an encryption algorithm to conceal command and control traffic rather than relying on any inherent protections provided by a communication protocol.

downloads additional powershell modules from it Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment.

This one-line powershell command Adversaries may abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution.

Powershell version of credential theft tool MimiKatz was also used by the actors to facilitate credential acquisition for lateral movement purposes. Adversaries may attempt to dump credentials to obtain account login and credential material.

It was executed via rundll32 commands Adversaries may bypass process and/or signature-based defenses by proxying execution of malicious content with signed, or otherwise trusted, binaries.

They also engaged in a number of antiforensic analysis measures, such as periodic event log clearing Adversaries may delete or modify artifacts generated within systems to remove evidence of their presence or hinder defenses.

annotator L.report_text:
COZY BEAR

COZY BEAR's preferred intrusion method is a broadly targeted spearphish campaign that typically includes web links to a malicious dropper. The technique describes sending spearphishing emails that contain links to a malicious dropper.

the code will deliver one of a number of sophisticated Remote Access Tools (RATs), including AdobeARM, ATI-Agent, and MiniDionis The technique describes the transfer of tools as RATs on the victim's machine.

the code will deliver one of a number of sophisticated Remote Access Tools (RATs), including AdobeARM, ATI-Agent, and MiniDionis The technique describes the use of remote access software to maintain control over infected systems., , and MiniDionis are examples of such tools.

not being analyzed on a virtual machine, using a debugger, or located within a sandbox The dropper and payload contain techniques to ensure that the sample is not analyzed on a virtual machine, using a debugger, or within a sandbox.

They have extensive checks for the various security software that is installed on the system and their specific configurations. Attackers perform extensive checks to identify the security software installed on the system and their specific configurations.

When specific versions are discovered that may cause issues for the RAT, it promptly exits When specific versions of security software are discovered that may cause problems for the RAT, the malware promptly closes to avoid detection or blocking.

The implants are highly configurable via encrypted configuration files Implants (implants) use encrypted configuration files to hide and protect sensitive information such as command and control (C2) servers, initial tasks, persistence mechanisms, and encryption keys.

An HTTP protocol with encrypted payload is used for the Command & Control communication Adversaries may communicate using OSI application layer protocols.

The COZY BEAR intrusion relied primarily on the SeaDaddy implant developed in Python and compiled with py2exe and another Powershell backdoor with persistence accomplished via Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) system, which allowed the adversary to launch malicious code automatically after a specified period of system uptime or on a specific schedule a single obfuscated command setup to run persistently via Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) system

It consists of a single obfuscated command setup to run persistently Adversaries may abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution.

It was executed via rundll32 commands rundll32 commands were used

an encrypted connection to C2 Adversaries may employ an encryption algorithm to conceal command and control traffic rather than relying on any inherent protections provided by a communication protocol.

downloads additional powershell modules from it, executing them in memory Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment.

Powershell version of credential theft tool MimiKatz was also used by the actors to facilitate credential acquisition for lateral movement purposes Adversaries may attempt to dump credentials to obtain account login and credential material.

They also engaged in a number of antiforensic analysis measures, such as periodic event log clearing (viawevtutil cl Systemand wevtutil cl Securitycommands) and resetting timestamps of files delete or modify artifacts generated within systems to remove evidence of their presence or hinder defenses
